does: This paper introduces Agent Security Bench (ASB), a comprehensive benchmarking framework that formalizes and evaluates security vulnerabilities in LLM-based agents. ASB encompasses ten real-world scenarios (e-commerce, autonomous driving, finance), ten agent implementations, over 400 tools, and includes 27 attack and defense methods with 7 evaluation metrics. The work benchmarks multiple attack types including prompt injection attacks, memory poisoning, a novel Plan-of-Thought backdoor attack, and mixed attacks against 13 LLM backbones. Results systematically identify critical vulnerabilities across different agent operation stages: system prompt handling, user input processing, tool usage, and memory retrieval. The benchmark reveals high average attack success rates and demonstrates varying effectiveness of different defense strategies.
connects: This work builds on growing research into adversarial attacks on language models and interactive systems. It extends earlier investigations of prompt injection vulnerabilities in LLMs by broadening the scope to complex agent architectures that combine multiple components—tool interfaces, memory systems, and planning mechanisms. The paper responds to the observation that while individual LLM vulnerabilities have been studied, comprehensive evaluation frameworks for multi-component agent systems remain limited. It combines insights from adversarial machine learning, software security testing, and LLM robustness research to create a unified evaluation methodology. The inclusion of diverse scenarios and agent types reflects lessons from prior work on evaluating safety across different deployment contexts and demonstrates the need for systematic benchmarking as agent complexity increases.
enables: This benchmark framework enables future research into developing robust defenses for LLM-based agents by providing standardized evaluation protocols and reproducible attack scenarios. It supports the development of more resilient agent architectures that account for vulnerabilities across multiple operational stages. The formalization of attack types and vulnerability patterns creates a foundation for designing targeted defense mechanisms that address specific agent components. Results can guide practitioners in hardening agents deployed in safety-critical domains like finance and autonomous systems. The framework also enables comparative analysis of different LLM backbones under adversarial conditions, potentially informing model selection for security-sensitive applications. Future work can extend ASB to emerging agent designs and new attack vectors, supporting the continuous evolution of agent security research.
